questions.q: For i.i.d. $X_i$ with $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X_1}]$, Cramér's theorem says the rate function is:
questions.hint: It is the Legendre–Fenchel transform of $\Lambda$.
questions.explain: Cramér's theorem states $\frac{1}{n}\log\mathbb{P}(\bar X_n\ge x)\to -I(x)$ where $I(x)=\Lambda^*(x)=\sup_\theta\{\theta x-\Lambda(\theta)\}$ is the Legendre transform of the log-MGF.

questions.q: Let $X_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Compute the Cramér rate function at $x=2$, i.e. $I(2)$.
questions.explain: $\Lambda(\theta)=\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X}]=\theta^2/2$. Maximising $\theta x-\theta^2/2$ in $\theta$ gives $\theta^*=x$ and $I(x)=x^2/2$. At $x=2$: $I(2)=2$.

questions.q: Cramér's theorem requires which condition on the distribution of $X_1$?
questions.hint: We need exponential moments to even define the Legendre transform.
questions.explain: The classical statement requires the log-MGF $\Lambda(\theta)$ to be finite in an open neighbourhood of the origin (the so-called Cramér condition). Heavy-tailed laws like Cauchy fail this and have no Cramér-style LDP.

questions.q: A rate function $I$ produced as a Legendre transform of a convex log-MGF must be:
questions.hint: Legendre transforms of convex functions are convex; the sup of affine functions is lsc.
questions.explain: Since $\Lambda(\theta)$ is convex, its Legendre transform $I=\Lambda^*$ is the supremum of affine functions $\theta\mapsto\theta x-\Lambda(\theta)$ — hence convex and lower semicontinuous. It need not be bounded or differentiable.

questions.q: For $X_i\sim\text{Bernoulli}(1/2)$, the rate function is $I(x)=x\log(2x)+(1-x)\log(2(1-x))$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $I(0.75)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals).
At which point does a rate function $I$ for the empirical mean of i.i.d.\ $X_i$ with mean $\mu$ vanish?
questions.hint: The LLN says $\bar X_n\to\mu$ — so deviations from $\mu$ are not large.
questions.explain: By the law of large numbers $\bar X_n\to\mu$ a.s., so $\mathbb{P}(\bar X_n\approx\mu)\to 1$ and the exponential rate vanishes. Equivalently, $\theta=0$ achieves the sup in $\theta\mu-\Lambda(\theta)$ since $\Lambda'(0)=\mu$.

questions.q: Sanov's theorem identifies the rate function for the empirical distribution $L_n$ of i.i.d.\ samples from $\mu$ as:
questions.explain: Sanov: $\mathbb{P}(L_n\in A)\asymp e^{-n\inf_{\nu\in A}H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)}$. Relative entropy / KL divergence is the unique rate function consistent with i.i.d.\ exponential families.

questions.q: Let $\mu=\text{Ber}(1/2)$ and $\nu=\text{Ber}(0.9)$. Compute the KL divergence $H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)$ in nats (round to 3 decimals).
Cramér's theorem can be recovered from Sanov's by which mechanism?
choices: The contraction principle applied to $\nu\mapsto\int x\,d\nu$
questions.hint: The empirical mean is a continuous functional of the empirical measure.
questions.explain:
The contraction principle: if $L_n$ satisfies an LDP with rate $J$ and $f$ is continuous, then $f(L_n)$ satisfies an LDP with rate $I(y)=\inf\{J(
u):f(
u)=y\}$. Applying $f(
u)=\int x\,d
u$ to Sanov recovers Cramér.

questions.q: The Gärtner–Ellis theorem extends Cramér by replacing the i.i.d.\ log-MGF with:
questions.hint: It is a scaled limit of log-MGFs.
questions.explain: Gärtner–Ellis assumes the limiting log-MGF $\Lambda(\theta)=\lim_n\tfrac{1}{n}\log\mathbb{E}[e^{n\theta\bar X_n}]$ exists, is finite, and is essentially smooth — then the LDP holds with rate $I=\Lambda^*$. Cramér is the i.i.d.\ special case.

questions.q: An additional regularity hypothesis Gärtner–Ellis requires (beyond existence of $\Lambda$) is:
choices: $\Lambda$ is essentially smooth (steep at the boundary of its effective domain)
questions.hint: Without steepness the lower bound can fail — exposed points become inaccessible.
questions.explain: Essential smoothness: $\Lambda$ is differentiable in the interior of its effective domain and $|\Lambda'(\theta)|\to\infty$ as $\theta$ approaches the boundary. This guarantees every point in the relevant range is exposed and the lower LDP bound holds.

questions.q: For a stationary AR(1) chain $X_n=\rho X_{n-1}+\xi_n$ with $\xi_n\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ i.i.d.\ and $\rho\in(-1,1)$, the limiting $\Lambda(\theta)$ for $\bar X_n$ equals $\theta^2/(2(1-\rho)^2)$. With $\rho=0.5$, compute $I(1)$.
Schilder's theorem gives an LDP for $\sqrt{\varepsilon}\,W$ (scaled Brownian motion) on path space with rate function:
questions.hint: It is the Cameron–Martin / Dirichlet energy of the path.
questions.explain: Schilder: $\mathbb{P}(\sqrt\varepsilon W\in\cdot)$ satisfies an LDP at speed $1/\varepsilon$ with rate $I(\phi)=\tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^T|\dot\phi|^2 dt$ on the Cameron–Martin space (and $+\infty$ otherwise). Most-likely paths minimise kinetic energy.

questions.q: Freidlin–Wentzell theory studies the small-noise SDE $dX^\varepsilon_t=b(X^\varepsilon_t)\,dt+\sqrt\varepsilon\,dW_t$. The most-likely exit path from a basin minimises:
questions.hint: Replace $\dot\phi$ in Schilder's rate by the deviation from the deterministic flow.
questions.explain: Freidlin–Wentzell: the rate function is $S(\phi)=\tfrac{1}{2}\int|\dot\phi-b(\phi)|^2\,dt$. Mean exit times grow like $\exp(V/\varepsilon)$ where $V$ is the quasi-potential — the action minimum over paths from the attractor to the boundary.

questions.q: Boltzmann's entropy formula $S=k\log W$ is recovered from large deviations as:
choices: Sanov's theorem applied to the empirical occupation distribution of an ideal gas — entropy is $-H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)$
A Fourier coefficient of the partition function
questions.hint: The Gibbs measure is the minimiser of free energy = energy minus entropy.
questions.explain: Sanov's rate $H(\nu\,\|\,\mu)$ is — up to sign and the Boltzmann constant — Boltzmann–Gibbs entropy. The most likely macrostate maximises entropy subject to energy constraints, recovering the canonical ensemble. This is the rigorous probabilistic foundation of statistical mechanics.
